elements.sent-2411-001: Coherent perfect absorption (CPA) \cite{cpa_prl, dutta_gupta_2012, dutta_gupta_2013, cpa_review_baranov,longhipra,timereversedlasing_science is a mechanism for achieving null scattering from a scatterer under multiple illuminations.}
elements.sent-2411-002: CPA has been extensively exploited in the classical optics regime across various structures, such as epsilon-near-zero (ENZ) and Kerr nonlinear materials \cite{nonlinearitysdg,NireekshanReddy,NireekshanReddyol , metasurfaces \cite{cpa_metamaterial_selective, cpa_metamaterial_polarization , and gratings \cite{cpa_grating_ol , as well as in the realm of quantum optics \cite{agarwalcpa with lossy beam splitters and graphene-based structures \cite{quantum_cpa_grapehne .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-003: CPA is the generalization of critical coupling \cite{critical_coupling_sdg,nonlinearitysdg which involves only one coherent source.}
elements.sent-2411-004: When applied to a beam, the CPA framework faces a critical limitation due to the oversight of the dispersion of the wavevectors constituting the beam.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, most analyses typically consider the beam as a single-plane wave characterized by a single wave vector, thereby simplifying the complexity of the phenomenon, and restricting applications.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this study, we invoke a generic angular spectrum formalism originally developed by Bliokh and Aiello \cite{Bliokh_2013 to understand the absorption characteristics of Gaussian and Laguerre-Gaussian (LG) beams incident normally on a planar absorptive slab.}
elements.sent-2411-007: We have enhanced this approach with two major improvements \cite{sounak_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: The strict paraxial approximation has been relaxed to accommodate illuminations with greater transverse momentum spread, and the exact Fresnel transmission $(t)$ and reflection $(r)$ coefficients have been used in place of the first-order Taylor expansion of the coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-009: Thus, our proposed method can address a wide variety of counter-propagating beams falling normally on an arbitrary stratified medium where the $r$ and $t$ coefficients are computed using the transfer matrix method \cite{gupta2015wave, sauvik_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: We now proceed to elaborate our approach and describe our formalism in detail.
elements.sent-2411-011: The schematic depicted in Fig. \ref{cpa_plane_wave (a) pictorially describes the CPA configuration involving beams.}
elements.sent-2411-012: In this method, starting from the respective $k$-space spectrum \cite{sounak_2024 of the Gaussian or LG beam, the polarization transformation of each spatial harmonic (i.e., each $k$-vector) is performed through rotational transformations determined by the local polar $(\theta_i)$ and azimuthal angles $(\phi_i)$ associated with each $k$-vector.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The final real space beam profiles are found by evaluating the inverse Fourier transforms of the scattered spectrum: $| \mathbf{E _a \rangle = U_a^\dag F_a U_i | \mathbf{E _i \rangle$.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Where, $| \mathbf{E _i \rangle$ is the incident spectrum, $U_a = \hat{R _y(\theta_a) \hat{R _z(\phi_a) \hat{R _y(\vartheta_a)$ and $F_a = \text{diag (\xi_p,\xi_s), \xi = r,t$ contains the Fresnel coefficients respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The subscript a = i, r, and t stand for the incident, reflected, and transmitted beams respectively.
elements.sent-2411-016: The exact expressions of the incident beam spectrums ($| \mathbf{E _i \rangle$) and the local spherical angles $(\theta_i,\phi_i)$ can be found in Appendix A \cite{sounak_2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: For convenience, we denote the forward (backward) propagating beam incident from the left (right) with subscripts '$f$' and '$b$' respectively.
elements.sent-2411-018: Since different wavevectors make different angles with the slab, it is straightforward to understand that all plane waves can not acquire the required phase difference and exhibit CPA simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-019: This physically indicates that there cannot be perfect absorption of the whole beam and the scattered beam will definitely have a structure.
elements.sent-2411-020: We have chosen a scenario where for the central $k$-vector, the amplitude of the reflected coefficient from the left ($|r_f|$) and the amplitude of the transmission coefficient from the right ($|t_b|$) are equal.
elements.sent-2411-021: They also possess a phase difference of $\pi$ for the $s$-polarized light and are in phase for the $p$-polarized beam.
elements.sent-2411-022: With this geometry, we examine various combinations of beams, incident normally on the composite slab.
elements.sent-2411-023: The most straightforward combination consists of two $s$-polarized beams: one arriving from the left and the other from the right, which is designated as the S-S combination.
elements.sent-2411-024: In contrast, the P-P combination involves two $p$-polarized beams.
elements.sent-2411-025: For circularly polarized beams, those of the same helicity can be categorized as either LCP-LCP or RCP-RCP, while those of opposite helicities can be classified as LCP-RCP or RCP-LCP.
elements.sent-2411-026: Notably, an RCP beam under reflection becomes LCP, and thus it can interfere only with the LCP beam incident from the other side.
elements.sent-2411-027: The same is true for other polarizations as well.
elements.sent-2411-028: Therefore, identical polarizations from both sides cannot lead to destructive interference.
elements.sent-2411-029: This is clearly demonstrated in our results.

elements.sent-2411-030: It is worth mentioning that, for the symmetric oblique incidence, the situation becomes complex primarily due to two factors.
elements.sent-2411-031: First, it is challenging to spatially overlap the reflected beam from the left and the transmitted beam from the right due to the spatial localization of the beams.
elements.sent-2411-032: Moreover, the Goos-Hanchen (GH) and Imbert-Fedorov (IF) shifts further complicate the issue.
elements.sent-2411-033: Second, the reflection and transmission coefficients in general do not comply with the CPA condition: $r_f=t_b$ practically minimizing the possibility of destructive interference.

elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1 \linewidth]{Figure3.pdf} \caption{(a)-(f) display the spectrums of the incident and the scattered beams. (a) shows the Gaussian spectrum of the incident beam, while (b) and (c) depict the scattered spectrums for S-S (or P-P, LCP-RCP, RCP-LCP) and LCP-LCP (or RCP-RCP) combinations. (g) The scattered beam for the S-S combination. The line plots in (i) along the principal diagonal (PD) and the counter diagonal (CD) highlight the nonuniformity in this scattered beam. (h) The scattered beam for the LCP-RCP (or RCP-LCP) combination develops a faint side lobe, which is further illustrated in (j).} \label{lg_beam}
elements.sent-2411-034: We have chosen a gold-silica (metal-dielectric) composite layer as the absorber where the localized plasmon resonances determine the nature of the loss i.e., $\text{Im (\epsilon_2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-035: The dispersion characteristics of this medium are described by the Bruggeman formula \cite{optical_metamaterial, dutta_gupta_2012 : \begin{equation}\epsilon_2=\frac{1}{4} \{ (3f_m-1)\epsilon_m + (3f_d-1)\epsilon_d \pm \sqrt{[(3f_m -1)\epsilon_m+(3f_d-1)\epsilon_d]^2 + 8\epsilon_m\epsilon_d}\}\end{equation} where, $f_m$ and $\epsilon_m$ ($f_d$ and $\epsilon_d$) are the volume fraction and permittivity of the metal (dielectric), respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-036: To ensure causality, it is conventional to select the square root value that ensures a positive imaginary part of the permittivity.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1 \linewidth]{Figure5.pdf} \caption{Polarization states across the cross-section of the scattered beam for the incident (a)–(c) Gaussian beams and (d)–(f) LG beams with $l = 1$. The backgrounds in (a)-(f) contain contrast-enhanced and semi-transparent intensity distributions corresponding to the combinations mentioned earlier. Degree of circular polarization, $S3(=2\text{Im}(E_x^*E_y))$, across the cross-section of the scattered beam for the incident (g)–(i) Gaussian beams and (j)–(l) LG beams with $l = 1$. Again, the beam combinations are S-S in (g) and (j), LCP-LCP in (h) and (k), and LCP-RCP in (i) and (l).} \label{polarization_state}

elements.sent-2411-078: Another key factor is the polarization state across the cross-section of the scattered beam.
elements.sent-2411-079: Due to interactions with the slab, the polarization generally loses its uniformity across the beam.

elements.sent-2411-084: In this case, typically, four lobes with opposite circular polarization, again dictated by the variation in diattenuation $\mathfrak{D}$, can be observed across the beam.

elements.sent-2411-086: For the LCP-RCP (RCP-LCP) type of combination, only the positive (negative) helicity regions can be seen across the scattered beam.
elements.sent-2411-087: Interestingly, for the circularly polarized light with the same helicity, the $p$-component of the polarization gets canceled and predominantly the $s$-polarization is left, so that the scattered beam is predominantly $s$-polarized.
elements.sent-2411-088: In this case, both positive and negative helicity regions can also be found juxtaposed in both Gaussian and LG beams (Figs.. \ref{polarization_state (i), (l)).}
elements.sent-2411-089: So far, beams with identical waists have been considered, specifically $w_0=10\lambda$.

elements.sent-2411-091: This size disparity alters the spectral spread, leading to near-destructive interference for plane waves with a fixed polar angle in $k$-space.
elements.sent-2411-092: Thus, even a slight difference in beam waists can significantly change the scattered beam structure, as shown in Appendix B for an S-S combination of Gaussian beams with $w_{0_{1 =10\lambda$ (left) and $w_{0_{2 =9.8\lambda$ (right).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: In conclusion, we have extended the concept of coherent perfect absorption (CPA) from plane waves to beams with a finite transverse extent.
elements.sent-2411-094: Using the angular spectrum method, we examined the impact of beam polarization when interacting with an absorptive slab under both single and dual-channel illumination.
elements.sent-2411-095: Our findings reveal that complete absorption of a beam is not achievable within this configuration, highlighting a limitation of the CPA mechanism specific to this platform.
elements.sent-2411-096: However, our approach based on classical wave optics offers potential for further exploration, such as extending the analysis to oblique incidence, illumination on photonic crystals, metasurfaces, nonlinear medium, or low-power applications.
elements.sent-2411-097: Moreover, the structured nature of the scattered beam opens up new possibilities for beam shaping.
elements.sent-2411-098: This could be achieved by employing two different beams incident from opposite sides, or by cascading the output of one absorptive slab to serve as the input for another, thus expanding the scope of CPA applications.
elements.section-2411-001: Appendix A: Spectrums and the local angles
elements.sent-2411-099: Normalized spectrum for a Gaussian beam \cite{sounak_2024, sounak_2023,bliokh2014extraordinary : \begin{equation}\label{gaussain_spectrum} |\mathbf{E}_i\rangle = \frac{w_0}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\{-(k_x^2+k_y^2)\omega_0^2/4\}(\mathbf{A}_p\hat{e}_p + \mathbf{A}_s\hat{e}_s)\end{equation} where $w_0$ is the beam waist, polarization description is encoded through Jone's vector components $\mathbf{A _p$ and $\mathbf{A _s$ of the beam for the $p$- and $s$- polarization respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The spectrum corresponding to the LG beam is \cite{sounak_2024, sounak_2023,bliokh2014extraordinary : \begin{equation}|\mathbf{E}_i\rangle = \frac{w_0}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{\{-(k_0\omega_0)^2\theta_z^2/4\}}\theta_z^{|l|}e^{il\phi+ik_0(1-\theta_z^2/2)z}(\mathbf{A}_p\hat{e}_p + \mathbf{A}_s\hat{e}_s)\end{equation} where, $\theta_z=\sqrt{k_x^2+k_y^2 /k_0$, $\phi=\tan^{-1 (k_y/k_x)$, and the azimuthal phase factor $e^{il\phi}$ contains the vortex charge $l=0,\pm1,\pm2,...$.}}}

(c) The real-space Gaussian beam incident from the left. (d) The corresponding scattered beam profile. (e)-(h) are the horizontal line plots of the profiles in (a)-(d).} \label{differentsizes}

elements.sent-2411-111: Coherent perfect absorption (CPA) \cite{cpa_prl, dutta_gupta_2012, dutta_gupta_2013, cpa_review_baranov,longhipra,timereversedlasing_science,gupta2015wave is a mechanism for achieving null scattering from a scatterer under multiple illuminations.}
elements.sent-2411-112: CPA has been extensively exploited across various structures, such as epsilon-near-zero (ENZ) and Kerr nonlinear materials \cite{nonlinearitysdg,NireekshanReddy,NireekshanReddyol , metasurfaces \cite{cpa_metamaterial_selective, cpa_metamaterial_polarization , and gratings \cite{cpa_grating_ol , as well as in the realm of quantum optics \cite{q1referee,q2referee,agarwalcpa with lossy beam splitters.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Note that earlier works \cite{q1referee,q2referee report precursor to CPA or CPA without naming it.}
elements.sent-2411-114: CPA is the generalization of critical coupling \cite{critical_coupling_sdg,nonlinearitysdg which involves only one input channel.}
elements.sent-2411-115: An early attempt to generalize the notion of CPA to scalar beams suffer from a description of the input beams with Gaussian profile only in the plane of incidence \cite{DEY2015515 predicting inaccurate results for oblique incidence.}
elements.sent-2411-116: In the context of single or multichannel inputs, there have been significant developments extending the notion of CPA to the case of any arbitrary superposition of plane waves \cite{arbitrary,ep,esurface,cpaep,arbwave .}
elements.sent-2411-117: These studies exploit exceptional points (EP) and exceptional surfaces (ES) or massively degenerate cavities requiring careful tuning of the specially prepared non-Hermitian coupled cavity systems without or with intracavity lenses.
elements.sent-2411-118: A scalar coupled mode theory was applied without adequate treatment of the polarization states of the incoming light.
elements.sent-2411-119: It is clear that the new degree of freedom introduced by the polarization of the incoming light (especially for beams carrying orbital angular momentum (OAM)) can render these advancements even richer by bringing in spin-orbit coupling effects like spin-Hall shifts into single or multichannel CPA involving both temporal and spatial frequency degeneracies.
elements.sent-2411-120: In view of the lack of a full vectorial theory, we reexamine the original CPA configuration by Wan et al \cite{timereversedlasing_science when the incident plane waves are replaced by well-defined vector beams with or without OAM.}

elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=1 \linewidth]{Figure2.pdf} \caption{The incident spectrum in (a) and the real-space (b) incident, (c) reflected, and (d) transmitted beams for single channel illumination from left -- all demonstrate a Gaussian character. The line plots in (e)-(h) in the second row further illustrate the Gaussian nature of the profiles in (a)-(d).} \label{single_channel}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.9 \linewidth]{Figure3.pdf} \caption{(a)-(f) display the spectrums of the incident and the scattered beams for dual channel illumination from both sides. (a) shows the Gaussian spectrum of the incident beam, while (b) and (c) depict the scattered spectrums for S-S and LCP-LCP combinations. The line plots in (d)-(f) further illustrate the characteristics of these spectrums presented in (a)-(c). Real-space beams are presented in (g)-(l): (g) is the incident Gaussian beam, (h) shows the scattered beam for the S-S combination, and (i) represents the LCP-LCP scattered beam. Line plots (j)-(l) along the $x$-axis depict the nature of the scattered beams corresponding to (g)-(i). Finally, (m) illustrates the incident beam with three different beam waists, while (n) shows the corresponding intensities of the scattered beams. } \label{gaussian_beam}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=1 \linewidth]{Figure4.pdf} \caption{(a) The spectrum and (b) the corresponding real LG beam with $l=1$. (c) Scattered beam spectrum for the S-S combination. The line plots in (d), (e), and (f) in the second row illustrate the characteristics of the spectrums or the beam presented in (a), (b), and (c), respectively. (g) The scattered beam for the S-S combination. The line plots in (i) along the principal diagonal (PD) and the counter diagonal (CD) highlight the nonuniformity in this scattered beam. (h) The scattered beam for the LCP-RCP combination develops a faint side lobe, which is further illustrated in (j).} \label{lg_beam}
elements.sent-2411-130: We have chosen a gold-silica composite layer as the absorber where localized plasmon resonances determine the nature of the loss i.e., $\text{Im (\epsilon_2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-131: The dispersion characteristics of this medium $(\epsilon_2)$ are described by the Bruggeman formula \cite{optical_metamaterial, dutta_gupta_2012 : \begin{equation}\epsilon_2=\frac{1}{4} \{ p\epsilon_m + q\epsilon_d \pm \sqrt{[p \epsilon_m+q\epsilon_d]^2 + 8\epsilon_m\epsilon_d}\}\end{equation} where, $p=3f_m-1$, $q=3f_d-1$, $f_m$ and $\epsilon_m$ ($f_d$ and $\epsilon_d$) are the volume fraction and permittivity of the metal (dielectric), respectively.}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.95 \linewidth]{Figure5.pdf} \caption{Polarization states across the cross-section of the scattered beam for the incident (a)–(c) Gaussian beams and (d)–(f) LG beams with $l = 1$. The beam combinations are S-S in (a) and (d), LCP-LCP in (b) and (e), and LCP-RCP in (c) and (f). The backgrounds in (a)-(f) contain contrast-enhanced and semi-transparent intensity distributions corresponding to the combinations mentioned earlier. Degree of circular polarization, $S3(=2\text{Im}(E_x^*E_y))$, across the cross-section of the scattered beam for the incident (g)–(i) Gaussian beams and (j)–(l) LG beams with $l = 1$. Again, the beam combinations are S-S in (g) and (j), LCP-LCP in (h) and (k), and LCP-RCP in (i) and (l).} \label{polarization_state}
elements.sent-2411-132: The dielectric function of gold, $\epsilon_m$, was derived through an interpolation of the experimental data provided by Johnson and Christy (more in supplementary note).
elements.sent-2411-133: Other material parameters are taken as follows: $\epsilon_1=\epsilon_3=1.0$ (permittivity of free space) and $\epsilon_d = 2.25$.
elements.sent-2411-134: Notably, beams with different sizes on two sides are of particular interest.
elements.sent-2411-135: One such example is provided in the supplementary note.
elements.sent-2411-136: In the P-P combination, the position of these high-intensity regions alters, appearing along the counter diagonal.
elements.sent-2411-137: This non-uniformity is a manifestation of azimuthal differential attenuation $(\mathfrak{D )$ for the orthogonal polarizations as observed in tight focusing \cite{basudebpra and scattering \cite{ngpaper .}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: On the contrary, for the circularly polarized beams, a predominantly circular state of polarization is found across the entire cross-section.
elements.sent-2411-139: Interestingly, for the circularly polarized light with the same helicity, the $p$-component of the polarization gets canceled and predominantly the $s$-polarization is left in the scattered beam.
elements.sent-2411-140: In conclusion, we have extended the work of \cite{timereversedlasing_science on CPA to include the effect of polarized input beams.}
elements.sent-2411-141: However, our approach offers potential for further exploration, such as extending the analysis to oblique incidence, illumination on photonic crystals, metasurfaces, nonlinear medium, or low-power applications.
elements.sent-2411-142: We believe that our study will motivate and necessitate the study of recent developments on CPA with ES, EP, and degenerate cavities with full vectorial beams.
